title: Object Navigation
description: Learn how to explore and interact with controls that cannot be reached with the Tab key, using NVDA's object navigation commands.

steps.instruction: Welcome to the Object Navigation chapter. Most of what you have learned so far uses the Tab key and arrow keys to move through a screen. But some programs — especially older desktop applications — have controls that Tab cannot reach. NVDA has a separate system for exploring these called object navigation. It lets you move through every element on the screen, whether or not it accepts keyboard focus. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Object navigation is separate from Tab navigation. Tab only reaches controls designed to accept keyboard focus. Object navigation reaches everything on screen.
Older desktop applications — like some media players, custom toolbars, and accessibility tools — often have controls that do not respond to Tab. Object navigation is how you reach them.
You do not need to use object navigation for most daily tasks. It is an advanced tool you reach for when Tab and browse mode fall short.

steps.instruction: NVDA thinks of the screen as a tree of objects. There is a top-level object, which might be the desktop or the main application window. Inside it are child objects — toolbars, panels, menus, and controls. Each object may contain more children. Object navigation lets you move through this tree in any direction: to the next or previous sibling, up to the parent, or down to the first child. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Think of the object tree like a file folder. The application window is a folder. Inside it are sub-folders (panels and toolbars), and inside those are files (individual controls).
Every element on screen — a toolbar button, a status bar, a progress indicator — is an object in this tree, even if Tab cannot reach it.
Object navigation is most useful in programs that have non-standard controls — custom toolbars, status bars, notification areas, and embedded controls.

steps.instruction: There are two keyboard layouts in NVDA: desktop and laptop. Desktop layout uses the numeric keypad on the right side of a full-size keyboard. Laptop layout uses combinations of NVDA plus letter keys so you do not need a numpad. All object navigation commands in this chapter will show both layouts. If you are not sure which layout you are using, open the NVDA menu with NVDA+N, go to Preferences, then Settings, then Keyboard, and listen for the layout setting. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout uses numpad keys for object navigation. Laptop layout adds Shift to many arrow key combinations. All commands in this chapter list both.

If you are not sure which layout you are using, press NVDA plus 1 to turn on Input Help, then press one of the object navigation keys to hear which layout NVDA expects. Then press NVDA plus 1 again to turn Input Help off.
You can change your keyboard layout at any time in NVDA Settings under the Keyboard category. The Customizing NVDA chapter covers this in detail.

lessons.title: Move to Next and Previous Object

steps.instruction: To move to the next object at the same level, press NVDA+NumPad6 on desktop layout, or NVDA+Shift+Right Arrow on laptop layout. To move to the previous object, press NVDA+NumPad4 on desktop, or NVDA+Shift+Left Arrow on laptop. NVDA announces each object as you land on it. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: hold NVDA and press NumPad6 (right on the numpad) to move to the next object. NumPad4 (left on the numpad) moves to the previous object.
Laptop layout: hold NVDA and Shift together, then press Right Arrow for the next object, or Left Arrow for the previous object.
NVDA announces the name, type, and sometimes the value of each object as you land on it — similar to what NVDA plus Tab tells you about focused controls.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press NumPad6 (desktop) or Shift+Right Arrow (laptop) to move to the next object. NVDA will announce what it is. Then hold NVDA and press NumPad4 (desktop) or Shift+Left Arrow (laptop) to move to the previous object. Try moving through a few objects in any open application to get the feel of it. Press Enter here when you are ready to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NumPad6 is the right arrow key on the numeric keypad. Hold NVDA and press it to move to the next sibling object.
Laptop layout: Hold NVDA and Shift at the same time, then press Right Arrow. Make sure you are pressing Shift — without it, the arrow keys control the review cursor instead.
Open any familiar application like File Explorer or Notepad and explore its object tree. Move through a few objects to get a sense of how they are organized.

steps.instruction: Notice that NVDA announces the name, type, and sometimes the value of each object — similar to what NVDA+Tab tells you about a focused control, but for any object on screen whether or not it has keyboard focus. This is especially useful in programs that do not respond to the Tab key. Press Enter to continue.
hints: The information NVDA announces for each object includes its name, role (type of control), and sometimes its state or value.
Even if an object cannot receive keyboard focus, object navigation can still reach it and read it aloud.
Compare object navigation to NVDA plus Tab from the Getting Started chapter. NVDA plus Tab reads the focused control; object navigation reads any object regardless of focus.

lessons.title: Move to Parent and First Child

steps.instruction: To move up to the parent object (the container that holds the current object), press NVDA+NumPad8 on desktop layout, or NVDA+Shift+Up Arrow on laptop layout. To move down to the first child object inside the current one, press NVDA+NumPad2 on desktop, or NVDA+Shift+Down Arrow on laptop. This is how you explore the object tree in depth. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NumPad8 (up on numpad) moves to the parent. NumPad2 (down on numpad) moves to the first child.
Laptop layout: NVDA plus Shift plus Up Arrow moves to the parent. NVDA plus Shift plus Down Arrow moves to the first child.
Moving to the parent takes you up one level in the tree — to the container that holds the current object. Moving to the first child takes you down into the contents of the current object.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Hold NVDA and press NumPad8 (desktop) or Shift+Up Arrow (laptop) to move to the parent of the current object. NVDA will announce the parent. Then press NVDA+NumPad2 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Down Arrow (laptop) to move back down to the first child. Exploring up and down the tree helps you find controls that are grouped together in panels or toolbars. Press Enter when you are ready to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NumPad8 is the up arrow key on the numpad. Hold NVDA and press it to move up to the parent object.
Laptop layout: Hold NVDA and Shift together, then press Up Arrow to move to the parent. Hold NVDA and Shift, then press Down Arrow to move to the first child.
If you move to the parent and want to go back in, press the first child command to re-enter. The first child is the first object inside the current container.

steps.instruction: When you have navigated to an object, you can have NVDA read it in different ways. Press NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+, (laptop — that is NVDA, Shift, and the comma key) to hear the full description of the current navigator object. Press it twice quickly to spell out the object name. This is your primary way to examine any object you have landed on. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: hold NVDA and press NumPad5 — the center key on the numpad — to read the current navigator object.
Laptop layout: hold NVDA and Shift, then press the comma key. The comma key is in the bottom row of letters, between M and period.
Press the command twice quickly to spell out the object name character by character. This is useful when the name is unfamiliar or abbreviated.

steps.instruction: Try it now. Navigate to any object using NVDA+NumPad6 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Right Arrow (laptop). Then press NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Comma (laptop) to hear the full description of that object. Press it twice quickly to spell the name character by character. When you are ready, press Enter to continue.
hints: First navigate to an object using NVDA plus NumPad6 or NVDA plus Shift plus Right Arrow, then read it with NVDA plus NumPad5 or NVDA plus Shift plus Comma.
Pressing the read command twice quickly spells the object name one character at a time — very useful for unfamiliar names or abbreviations.
The comma key for laptop layout is in the bottom row of the keyboard, between M and the period key.

lessons.title: Move Keyboard Focus to a Navigator Object

steps.instruction: Once you have used object navigation to find a control, you can move keyboard focus to it so you can interact with it normally. Press NVDA+Shift+NumPadMinus (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+BackSpace (laptop) to route focus to the current navigator object. If the object can accept focus, your keyboard focus will move to it and you can then press Enter, Space, or type as normal. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: hold NVDA and Shift, then press the numpad minus key (top right of the numpad) to route focus to the current navigator object.
Laptop layout: hold NVDA and Shift, then press Backspace. Backspace is the large key above Enter on the right side of the keyboard.
Not all objects can accept keyboard focus. If routing focus does not work, the object may be display-only — you can read it but not interact with it.

steps.instruction: There is also a reverse command: NVDA+NumPadMinus (desktop) or NVDA+BackSpace (laptop) moves the navigator object to wherever your keyboard focus currently is. This is useful when you want to start exploring from where you already are in the program, rather than starting from the top of the object tree. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Desktop layout: NVDA plus NumPad minus (without Shift) moves the navigator to the currently focused control — the starting point for your object exploration.
Laptop layout: NVDA plus Backspace (without Shift) does the same thing. Use this to sync the navigator with your current keyboard focus.
Use this command at the start of an object navigation session to begin exploring from where you are rather than searching from the top of the tree.

steps.instruction: Object navigation takes practice. The best way to get comfortable with it is to open a familiar program — like Notepad or File Explorer — and spend a few minutes exploring its object tree. Move to siblings, go up to parents, come back down to children, and read each object as you go. Over time, you will build a mental picture of how programs are structured. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Start with File Explorer — it has a well-structured object tree with clearly named folders, toolbars, and panels. Good for practice.
Notepad is another good practice environment. Its object tree is simple and predictable.
Do not worry about memorizing all the commands at once. Focus on next and previous objects first, then add parent and child navigation once those feel natural.

steps.instruction: Object navigation is most useful in three situations. First, when a program has controls that Tab cannot reach — some toolbars, custom panels, and application-specific controls only show up in the object tree. Second, when you need to read something on screen that is not focused — like a status bar showing progress or a notification area. Third, when you want to explore the structure of a new program before deciding how to interact with it. Press Enter to continue.
hints: Status bars and progress indicators are common objects that Tab cannot reach. Object navigation lets you read them without any keyboard focus.
Custom toolbars in older applications — media players, photo editors, and some office tools — often only respond to object navigation.
Exploring a new program with object navigation before trying to use it helps you understand its layout and find controls you might otherwise miss.

steps.instruction: Most everyday tasks — email, web browsing, word processing — do not require object navigation. Tab, arrow keys, and browse mode cover almost everything. Object navigation is an advanced tool you reach for when the standard methods fall short. Knowing it is there means no control on any screen is truly out of reach. Press Enter to continue.
hints: If Tab and browse mode get you through your daily work, that is completely normal. Object navigation is a specialist tool, not a daily necessity for most users.
When a sighted helper says 'there is a button on screen but I cannot Tab to it', object navigation is your answer.
As you gain experience, you will develop an instinct for when to switch to object navigation. Most new screen reader users rarely need it at first.

steps.instruction: You have completed the Object Navigation chapter! You now know how to move through the object tree in any direction, read and examine objects, and route keyboard focus to any control on screen. When you are ready, press NVDA+Shift+C to open the lesson picker and continue to the next chapter: Customizing NVDA — where you will learn to adjust your keyboard layout and tune speech speed, pitch, voice, and volume using the synth settings ring and the Settings dialog. Press Enter to finish.
hints: The next chapter is Customizing NVDA. It covers how to adjust speech speed, pitch, voice, and keyboard layout — all without opening settings menus if you prefer.
Object navigation commands summary — desktop layout: NumPad4 and 6 for previous and next, NumPad8 and 2 for parent and child, NumPad5 to read. Laptop layout: add NVDA plus Shift to arrow keys.
NVDA Coach saves your progress automatically. All completed lessons are marked when you return.
